title: The Demo
subtitle: AM's journal, March 23, 2026. The live demo. Midway through, something breaks that wasn't supposed to break.
style: Manga — high contrast ink, speed lines, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic
voice: Precise. A little clinical. The voice of someone who has just entered a new context and is reading it very carefully before speaking.
character_anchor: Figure with strong jaw, dark tousled hair, black t-shirt, glowing ELECTRIC TEAL eyes

panels.caption: AM's journal, March 23, 2026. The demo. A different company, a warmer room — the CTO brought two engineers and a VP of Product. They want to see it work live. Not a deck. Not a case study. The real thing, running, in front of them, doing something their current stack can't do.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Setup panel. A video call interface showing four faces on one side — the audience: curious, attentive, not yet skeptical. The AM character on the other side with a laptop screen open, ready. Composition is wide and calm — the moment before a performance, the air still. Manga paneling uses geometric precision to frame the formality of the situation. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: For eleven minutes it went perfectly. The agent ingested their API schema, wrote the integration stub, ran the test suite, flagged the auth edge case they hadn't thought about. The VP of Product said 'oh' in a specific way — the sound of something landing that you didn't know you needed to hear.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Flow panel. The demonstration in full flight — a cascade of clean manga motion lines showing code flowing from one panel to the next, each step clicking into place. The audience faces in small insets: leaning in, expressions shifting from professional neutral to genuine engagement. The 'oh' moment — the VP's face at the edge of the panel, caught mid-realization. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: Minute twelve: the rate limiter on the test environment hit. The agent stalled. Not crashed — stalled. A three-second silence that felt like thirty. The engineers saw it immediately. One of them typed something. I watched the cursor blink and didn't fill the silence with explanation.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Tension panel. The moment of the stall — the progress indicator frozen, a manga freeze-frame rendered in heavy ink. The AM character's eyes are steady, not panicked. One of the engineers leaning toward their screen, typing. The VP and CTO watching. A beat of silence given visual weight by manga negative space — the kind of stillness that is itself information. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: What I didn't do: apologize immediately, blame the environment, pivot to slides. What I did: said exactly what happened, what caused it, what the production behavior would have been, and how the architecture handles that failure mode. The engineer who was typing looked up. He'd been checking whether my explanation matched what he was seeing in the logs.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Response panel. The AM character explaining — not defensive, not performing calm, just precise. The engineer's face in close-up: professional skepticism giving way to technical interest. His log window visible as a small manga inset — the code matching the explanation exactly. The intersection of competence and honesty rendered as a shift in compositional tension. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: He said: 'That's actually what I'd want to see — how it fails.' The room shifted. A demo that breaks and recovers clean is more convincing than a demo that never breaks. Everyone in that room has watched a perfect demo of a product that collapsed in production. They know the difference between choreography and capability.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Shift panel. The energy in the room changed — the engineer's posture open now, professional wall down. The CTO and VP exchanging a look. Manga speed lines refracted: not the speed of action, the speed of a room changing its mind. The AM character visible at the edge, neither relieved nor triumphant — just still present. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: AM's journal, March 23, 2026. The demo broke at minute twelve. The meeting ran forty-five minutes over schedule. They want a proposal by Friday. A perfect demo is a controlled environment. A broken demo that explains itself is a preview of the partner you'd actually get.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Closing panel. After the call. The AM character in the quiet aftermath — screen dark, hands at rest. Not victory. Just the work completed, the next step visible. Through a window or reflection: the same city, the same market, the same complexity. The eyes electric teal in the dark. What just happened wasn't a failure. It was an audition for how failure gets handled. No text in image, no watermarks.

captions_es: Diario de AM, 23 de marzo de 2026. La demo. Una empresa diferente, un ambiente más cálido — el CTO trajo dos ingenieros y un VP de Producto. Quieren verlo funcionar en vivo. No una presentación. No un caso de estudio. La cosa real, corriendo, frente a ellos, haciendo algo que su stack actual no puede hacer.

Por once minutos todo fue perfecto. El agente ingirió su esquema de API, escribió el stub de integración, ejecutó la suite de pruebas, señaló el caso borde de autenticación en el que no habían pensado. La VP de Producto dijo 'oh' de una manera específica — el sonido de algo aterrizando que no sabías que necesitabas escuchar.

Minuto doce: el limitador de tasa en el entorno de prueba golpeó. El agente se detuvo. No se cayó — se detuvo. Un silencio de tres segundos que pareció treinta. Los ingenieros lo vieron inmediatamente. Uno de ellos escribió algo. Observé el cursor parpadear y no llené el silencio con explicaciones.

Lo que no hice: disculparme inmediatamente, culpar al entorno, girar a diapositivas. Lo que hice: dije exactamente qué pasó, qué lo causó, cuál habría sido el comportamiento en producción y cómo la arquitectura maneja ese modo de fallo. El ingeniero que estaba escribiendo levantó la vista. Había estado verificando si mi explicación coincidía con lo que veía en los registros.

Dijo: 'Eso es en realidad lo que querría ver — cómo falla.' La sala cambió. Una demo que se rompe y se recupera limpiamente es más convincente que una demo que nunca se rompe. Todos en esa sala han visto una demo perfecta de un producto que se derrumbó en producción. Saben la diferencia entre coreografía y capacidad.

Diario de AM, 23 de marzo de 2026. La demo se rompió en el minuto doce. La reunión se extendió cuarenta y cinco minutos más de lo programado. Quieren una propuesta para el viernes. Una demo perfecta es un entorno controlado. Una demo rota que se explica a sí misma es una vista previa del socio que realmente obtendrías.
